    • One tael each of Chuan Huanglian, Digupi, Ganjiang, and Yanhusuo

    One black-feathered, black-boned rooster, fully grown, with the feathers and intestines removed. Use four taels each of Ai Ye and Qinghao, place half inside the chicken's abdomen, then put the chicken and the remaining herbs into a jar. Soak with a mixture of young boy's urine and water, about two inches over the chicken, and boil until soft. Remove and debone, then dry the meat.

    The above mixture is ground into a fine powder, and chicken juice is mixed to form pills the size of a walnut. Take fifty to sixty pills with warm wine or rice drink. Avoid stir-fried amaranth. If there are pains in the muscles or bones, remove the meat and use the bones. If the menstrual flow is early, add Huangqin, Huanglian, and Digupi. If the flow is late, add Ren, Zhu, and Huangqi. For white discharge, add Erzhu, Xiangfu, Shengma, Baizhi, and Chaihu. A rooster with white silk feathers and a tall crown is particularly excellent; it must be raised separately, and its Huangqi must be stir-fried and made into pills for feeding, avoiding contact with hens.[52p]
    Small Black Chicken Pills

    For the same treatment as above.

    Ingredients: Wu Yu, ginger (both good and white), dong quai, peony, yan hu suo (corydalis), po gu zhi, Sichuan pepper, dried tangerine peel, green tangerine peel, liu ji nu, raw earth, galangal, and Sichuan chuanxiong, each one tael; lotus leaf ash, four taels; northern mugwort, two taels; black chicken meat.

    Grind these into a powder, then cook the black chicken meat until tender and form into pills the size of a large walnut. Mix with fifty pills and cool with rice wine. If there is abdominal pain with black blood, add fried huang lian (coptis). For phlegm-dampness, add nan xing (arisaema), 苍朮, and fragrant cyperus. If there's red discharge, consume with clear tea. For intestinal wind, drink with aged rice water mixed with hundred herb frost. For heart pain, take with calamus wine. If there’s a miscarriage with bleeding, take with black plum wine. For deafness, drink with aged tea. For pain in the lower back and legs, take with dong quai wine. For headaches, drink with mint decoction. For black visual disturbances due to blood wind, take with licorice decoction. For sores, drink with raw rehmannia decoction. For body aches, mix astragalus powder with wine. For pain caused by air and blood clots, drink with white scallion broth. For white dysentery before and after childbirth, drink with ginger decoction; for red dysentery, drink with licorice decoction. For all hundred diseases, drink with vinegar broth.[53p]
    Qinxin Pill: Treats spleen dampness flowing down to the kidneys, combining with the fire of the elements to create damp heat, leading to leakage of the menstrual blood, characterized by a purple-black and foul odor. Use two taels of willow bark, soak in vinegar for seven days, then dry and roast. Repeat this soaking and roasting seven times. Grind into a powder and form into pills the size of a Wutong nut, taking seventy pills with warm wine.

    Siwu Kanli Pill: Treats the same condition.

    Ingredients: One and a half taels of fresh Rehmannia, the same amount of cooked Rehmannia soaked in wine and then mashed into a paste, each two taels of Angelica and Chinese Peony (one and a half taels of each), stir-fried in wine. One tael each of Anemarrhena and Coptis, soaked in wine and stir-fried. One tael each of cedar leaves and sophora seeds, both stir-fried. Six qian of Forsythia. Grind these ingredients into a powder, form into pills the size of a Wutong nut, and store them in a ceramic dish with the mouth sealed with paper. Leave in a cool place for seven days to eliminate fire toxins, then sun-dry and store. Take three to four dozen to five or six dozen pills with warm water or wine.[54p]
    Gujing Pills: For the same conditions as above.
    Huangqin, Baishao, and Turtle shell each one liang; Chunken root bark seven qian; Huangbo three qian; Xiangfu two and a half qian. Grind these into a powder, mix with wine to form pills the size of walnuts, take fifty pills with wine.

    Shengyang Tiaojing Decoction: For exhaustion from diet and labor, persistent excessive bleeding, or discharge of water, laziness and sleepiness, fatigue in the limbs, and menstrual discharge in summer.
    Duhuo five fen, Manjingzi seven fen, Danggui, Fangfeng, Gancao, Shengma, and Gao Ben each one qian; Chaihu, Qianghuo, Cangzhu, Huangqi each half qian. Boil with water on an empty stomach and take with rice.

    Shengyang Jujing Decoction: For the same conditions as above.
    Rougui, Baishao, and Honghua each five fen; Xixin six fen; Renshen, Gan Shudi Huang, and Chuanxiong each one qian; Duhuo, Heifuzhu, and Gancao each half qian; Qianghuo, Gao Ben, and Fangfeng each two qian; Baizhu, Danggui, Huangqi, and Chaihu each three qian; ten Tao Ren. Divide into four doses, boil with water on an empty stomach. In summer, do not use Rougui.

    Wujisan: For wind-cold blood loss, adjust the dosage as needed.
    Baizhi, Chuanxiong, Shaoyao, Gancao, Fuling, Danggui, and Rougui each three fen; Chenpi and Mahuang each six fen; Houpu and Ganjiang each four fen; Jiegeng one and a half fen; Zhiqiao, stir-fried, five fen; Banxia two fen; Cangzhu seven and a half fen. Boil and take.[55p]
    Not changing the Golden Vitality Powder - treats cold-induced blood collapse.
    Ingredients: 1 qian of Magnolia Officinalis, dried Tangerine Peel, Agastache, Pinellia, and Atractylodes; 5 fen of Licorice; additionally, 3 slices of Ginger and 2 dates, boiled in water and taken warm.

    Rising Yang and Removing Damp Decoction - treats excess dampness causing blood collapse.
    Ingredients: 1 fen of Atractylodes, 5 fen each of Siberian Motherwort, Bupleurum, Siler, Fermented Sweet Rice, Alisma, and Polyporus; 3 fen each of Dried Tangerine Peel, Licorice, and Malt; additionally, boiled with Ginger and dates.

    Tangerine Return Pill - treats constant blood collapse.
    Ingredients: 4 taels of Tangerine Peel and 3 taels of Angelica, powdered, made into honey pills the size of a Chinese wolfberry, taking 50 pills with warm wine. An alternate recipe adds Corydalis.

    Black Spirit Powder - treats women’s bleeding disorders.
    Ingredients: equal parts of Bai Cao Shuang and Bai Zhi, powdered. For use, take 2 qian, boiled with a little children’s urine and rice vinegar.

    Female Golden Elixir - treats middle bleeding and discharges.
    Ingredients: 1 tael each of White Peony, Angelica, Ligusticum, Ginseng, Atractylodes, Poria, Gleditsia, Bai Zhi, Bai Wei, Cassia, Corydalis, and Peony Root, soaked in wine for three days and nights, then sun-dried; 5 fen each of Myrrh and Licorice, and 1 jin of Cyperus soaked in vinegar. Powdered, made into honey pills the size of a Chinese wolfberry, taking 50 pills with warm wine.[56p]
    Er Yi Wan - For post-hemorrhage recovery, use this with adjustments.
    Angelica and Rehmannia radices in equal parts, steamed with wine seven times.
    Then, mix with honey to form pills the size of a walnut. Take 70 pills with empty stomach and wine.

    Bu Zhong Yi Qi Tang - Treats weak spleen and stomach after bleeding.
    Qiang Huo 1 qian, Fang Feng 7 fen, Gan Cao 5 fen, Huang Lian and Huang Qin 3 fen each, Ren Shen 5 fen, Huang Qi 7 fen. Boil and take.

    Ping Bu Zhen Xin Dan - For treating women's white bleeding.
    Fu Ling, Fu Shen (wood removed), Wu Wei Zi, Che Qian Zi, Rou Gui, Mai Men Dong, each 1.5 liang; Yuan Zhi, Shan Yao, Tian Men Dong, cooked Rehmannia each 1.5 liang; Suan Zao Ren 2.5 qian, Long Chi 2.5 liang, Ren Shen and Zhu Sha each 5 qian. Grind to powder, make into pills the size of a walnut, take 30 pills with rice drink.

    Bai Zhi San - For bleeding with discharge.
    Bai Zhi 1 liang, Hai Piao Xiao (burnt) 2 pieces,胎发烧灰 (burnt fetal hair) 1 piece. Grind to powder, take 2 qian with wine. Alternatively, use 6 liang of Bai Zhi, soak with half a jin of lime for three days, wash away the ash, fry the Bai Zhi until charred, add 4 liang of pepper and a little madder root if desired to powder, take with porridge pills to treat white discharge during bleeding.[57p]
    Ningsheng Gao treats anxiety after hemorrhage.
    Incense, realgar, each five qian; sour jujube seed, ginseng, poria, each one liang; amber seven and a half qian. Grind the above into a powder and prepare a decoction with lantern heart and jujube, taking one qian; or form into honey pills the size of a pellet, using peppermint decoction to dissolve and take one pill.

    Ziyin Ningshen Decoction treats the same conditions.
    Angelica, chuanxiong, white peony, cooked rehmannia, ginseng, poria, white atractylodes, and distant will each one qian; sour jujube seed and licorice each five fen; stir-fried coptis, four fen. Prepare this and take. If there is phlegm, add one qian of southern star.

    Dawenjing Decoction treats excessive blood loss during hemorrhage and irregular menstruation.
    Donkey-hide gelatin, peony, chuanxiong, angelica, ginseng, peony root, cinnamon, wuzhuyu, and licorice each two fen; half-summer two and a half fen; mai men dong five fen. Prepare and take.

    Wuyao Decoction treats internal activation of yang qi, resulting in hemorrhage in the chest and frequent blood in urine.
    Wuyao one and a half qian, cyperus two qian, angelica one qian, wood incense and licorice each five fen. Prepare and take on an empty stomach.

    Gui Zhi Decoction, an ancient formula for hemorrhage, can be adjusted to strengthen defensive qi.
    Cinnamon twig three qian, licorice one qian, white peony three qian.[58p]
    Right, boil ginger and jujube for consumption.

    Lizhengtang: An ancient formula for treating uterine bleeding; often modified to dry dampness and strengthen the spleen. Ingredients: 1.5 qian of licorice, 2 qian each of ginseng, white atractylodes, and dried ginger. Right, boil ginger and jujube for consumption.

    Yimu Gao: Treats excessive blood loss. Use motherwort, harvested on the Dragon Boat Festival, with purple flowers and straight stems, clean and wash the roots. Crush in a stone mortar, filter the concentrated juice through cloth into a clay pot, and simmer over gentle heat until it reaches the color of brown sugar. Store in a ceramic jar. Take one spoon each time, diluted with jujube decoction. Another method uses dried motherwort, avoids iron powder, forms into honey pills the size of bullet beads, and takes one pill with jujube decoction.

    Ersheng Tang: Treats uterine bleeding effectively. Ingredients: Five qian of polygonum multiflorum cut, three qian of licorice. Use a bowl of yellow wine to boil until reduced by two-thirds, add a cup of the juice from spiny nightshade and consume together for immediate effect.

    Heilong Wan: Specifically for uterine bleeding and excessive menstrual flow; particularly effective. Burn black donkey dung to preserve its potency, grind into powder, and make into pills with dough. Each time, take seventy pills, swallowed with empty stomach yellow wine.

    Collection of Ancient and Modern Books on Medicine, Volume 396

    Women's Menstrual Disorders Formulas

    Duanyuan San: Treats uterine bleeding like a spring gushing forth without stopping. Cook the cottonseed in a copper vessel until smoke emits.[59p]
    The above is a prescription. Take two qian each time, mixed with warm yellow rice wine.

    Jingjie Siwu Decoction: Used to treat initial stages of continuous bleeding, effective regardless of whether it is due to deficiency or excess. Take it and the bleeding will stop. Ingredients: Jingjie, Chaihu, Danggui, Chuanxiong, Baishao, Shengdi, Xiangfu. Grind them, decoct and drink warm. If bleeding does not stop, consider adding Fangfeng, Shengma, fried Puhuang, and Baizhu. One recipe adds Dihuang which is quite effective. Another recipe adds fried Aiye and fried Ejiao, omitting Xiangfu and Jingjie.

    Ziqin Pill: Used for women over forty-nine years old when menstruation should cease but instead occurs monthly, or excessively and continuously, gradually leading to continuous bleeding. Ingredients: Chaihu, soaked and wrapped in paper, steamed seven times; two liang of Danggui, washed with wine. Grind them into a powder and form into pills the size of apricot seeds. Take five to seventy pills, washed down with empty stomach with plums and wine, three doses daily. Adding two liang of Xiangfu made with vinegar is especially effective.

    Danggui Longgu Pill: Used to treat irregular menstruation with excessive menstrual flow, lingering discharge, and various conditions of red and white discharge, as well as postpartum issues of persistent discharge. Ingredients: One liang each of Longgu and Huangbai; five qian each of Danggui, Baishao, Baifuling, Huaihua, and fried Aiye; two and a half qian of Muxiang. Grind into powder, form into pills the size of walnuts, and take seventy to eighty pills with rice soup. One variation adds five qian each of Huangqin and Baizhu for cumulative effect.[60p]
    Dúxíng Sàn (also known as Wǔlíngzhī Sàn or Chōudāo Sàn) is used to treat abdominal pain and persistent menstrual bleeding. The ingredients include half raw and half fried Wǔlíngzhī, ground to a fine powder, with two qian of boy's urine added.

    Bái Fán Wán is used for prolonged bleeding, combining ground white alum and burnt yellow dog skull, each four ounces, with two ounces of Xiāngfù. These are milled into a powder, formed into pill-sized balls with the consistency of a tung seed, and taken in doses of thirty pills with clear broth on an empty stomach. For treating sudden bleeding and blood loss, use nine ounces of stir-fried silkworm sand, one ounce of Ājiāo, and half an ounce of Fùlónggān, mixed into a fine powder and taken with warm wine, two to three qian on an empty stomach as needed.

    Zhènlíng Dān (also known as Zǐjīndān) treats women's conditions of insufficient qi and blood, excessive bleeding, and cold discharge, as well as infertility. Ingredients include one ounce of powdered cinnabar, separately ground乳香 (benzoin), two ounces of Wǔlíngzhī, and two ounces of Myrrh (both washed and free of stones); two ounces of quartz; Yǔyúliáng, fire-calcinated and steamed until crumbly (no specific number of times), processed similarly to Dàizhǐ.

    The above four ingredients are made into small blocks and placed in a small pot with salt and mud mixture, dried, and heated over ten pounds of charcoal until glowing red, allowing the fire to expire. After burying in the ground for two days to remove toxins, the mixture is finely powdered. Using glutinous rice flour, make paste balls the size of a chicken's head, sundry until dry and remove from heat. Each ball should be taken with vinegar soup on an empty stomach. Do not take if pregnant.[61p]
    San Hui San - Treats excessive uterine bleeding.
    Dry the branches of the side cypress lightly, five qian; burn the paulownia seeds into charcoal and reduce to ash, two qian; burn the palm leaves until semi-charred, taking care not to turn into white ash, three qian. Grind the above into a fine powder and divide into two doses, to be taken with a drink of glutinous rice water.

    Quan Sheng Huo Xue Tang (Eastern Garden) - Treats severe uterine bleeding with symptoms of faintness and loss of consciousness.
    White peony root and bulbus elevatae each one qian; fang feng, qiang huo, du huo, chai hu, gui shen, ge gen, and gan cao all seven fen; man jing zi and xi xin each three fen; hong hua one fen; gao ben and chuan xiong each five fen; sheng di huang and shu di huang each four fen. Grind the above into a powder, make a decoction with water and take.

    Qi Hui San (New Book) - Treats continuous uterine bleeding.
    Lotus seed pods, yi mu cao, dry lotus grass, poppy pods, salted crabs, palm leaves, and lotus stems, all in equal parts, burn until semi-charred. Grind into a fine powder and mix with vinegar point soup, take three qian.[62p]
    Jia Jian Shi Quan Da Bu Tang: For treating blood collapse and Qi deficiency. Ingredients: Shi Quan Da Bu without cinnamon, stir-fried black dried ginger, and Ejiao pearls as needed. Prepare by decocting.

    Wu Huai San: Treats all forms of blood loss and uterine bleeding. Ingredients: Lotus seed shells, yellow silk, tangled hair, Bai Cao powder, palm bark, all burnt to ash in equal parts; fried Zhi Zi, fried Pu Huang, pine soot ink, and blood-resisting substance. Grind to a fine powder. Take 9 grams with fresh lotus root juice or fresh radish juice; alternatively, make into honey pills and swallow 50 with rice drink.

    Jie Du Si Wu Tang: Also called Wen Qing Yin. Treats bleeding disorders, pale face, and abdominal pain. Ingredients: Huang Qin, Huang Lian, Huang Yao, Zhi Zi, raw dry Rehmannia, Dang Gui, Bai Shao, and Chuan Xiong, each 3 grams. Grind, make into a poultice, and decoct with water. This formula combines Huang Lian Jie Du Tang and Si Wu Tang.

    Yang Family's Black Gold Powder: Treats women’s blood and Qi deficiency, menstrual irregularities, and excessive bleeding. Ingredients: 30 grams of carp skin, yellow cow horn (horn thinking), palm bark, broken old paper, tangled hair, 30 grams of fish bones, stir-fried dry ginger, roasted Rehmannia, Dang Gui (washed and baked), and Horsetail, each in half a tael. Grind and mix together, place in a ceramic jar, seal with salt mud until dry, calcine over charcoal fire for 5 jin (about 2.5 kg) until the smoke is gone, bury in soil to cool, then grind finely. Take 6 grams with a little musk and rice drink on an empty stomach.[63p]
    Universal Plaster

    For treating weakness of the vital energy, women's red and white discharges, coldness in the uterus, and excessive menstrual bleeding, apply to the lower abdomen and knead one hundred and twenty times.

    Ingredients:

    • 4 pounds of fragrant oil
    • 1 ounce of white atractylodes
    • 1 ounce of red peony
    • 1 ounce of rhubarb
    • 1 ounce of coptis
    • 1 ounce of white peony
    • 1 ounce of two-headed sparganium
    • 50 pieces of black aconite, shelled
    • 2 ounces of root of jujube
    • 1 ounce of chuanxiong (Szechuan lovage)
    • 1 ounce of fresh rehmannia
    • 1 ounce of chuanjiao (Szechuan pepper)
    • 1 set of fetal hair for male offspring
    • 1 piece of pangolin
    • 2 ounces of cooked rehmannia
    • 1 ounce of apricot kernel
    • 1 ounce of locust bean
    • 1 ounce of huangbo (yellow skin) with rough skin removed

    Soak each ingredient in 50 shelled seeds of babao (a type of seed), and 120 whole seeds of purgative are shelled and sliced. Soak them in oil for 5 days in spring, 3 days in summer, 7 days in autumn, and 10 days in winter. Strain the oil from the pot, boil until thick and black, filter out sediment, add clean oil to the pot, and simmer over gentle heat until water droplets turn into pearls. Gradually add yellow lead oxide, stir continuously with two branches each of locust tree, willow, peach, and apricot, keep stirring while reducing heat slightly. Then add fragrant wood and allow to cool a bit before mixing in fine powders evenly. Store in a good bottle. Soak in water for seven days to remove the internal heat. When using, place in boiling water until it dissolves and spreads.

    Finely ground ingredients:

    • 5 qian of pearls
    • 1 ounce of ferulic acid
    • 1 ounce each of clove and agarwood
    • 3 ounces each of musk, dragon's blood, catechu, myrrh, and frankincense
    • 3 qian of amber

    Grind the above into a powder and mix into the previous plaster.[64p]
    Jian Huang San: Used to treat women's excessive bleeding.
    Jian Huang (cocoon yellow) consists of silkworm molted paper, burned for essence; late silkworm sand; white dead silkworm, stir-fried, each in equal parts; a small amount of musk. Grind these into a powder. Take two qian (a unit of weight) each time, consumed with rice water, three times a day for best effect.

    Gu Jing Wan (from "Zhun Zheng"): Treats persistent uterine bleeding.
    Artemisia leaves sautéed in vinegar, deer antler frost, Fuling liver, and dried ginger, all in equal parts. Grind these into a powder, mix with melted deer antler glue, form warm pills, and take fifty pills after meals with diluted vinegar soup.

    Xuan Fu Hua Tang (Zhongjing): Used for postpartum excessive bleeding with a pulse that is string-like and large.
    Three liang of Xuanfu flower, fourteen stalks of green onion, and a small amount of new jiang (pigment).
    Combine these three ingredients, add three sheng of water, boil down to one sheng, and take it all at once.[65p]
    Banxia Pill
    [From "Zhi Zhi Fang"]
    Treats vomiting of blood, irregular bleeding with discharge, phlegm, breathlessness, and fullness from edema, as well as eliminates accumulated ailments.
    Grind half summer into a fine powder, mix with ginger juice, wrap in a dough made of white flour, roast until golden, then remove the dough to retain half summer.
    Make it into pills the size of mung beans with rice paste; take ten pills with warm water. It is also said to use half a dose of Xiong Gui Decoction and Chen Xiang Jiang Qi Decoction mixed, taken to stop bleeding.

    Bei Jin San
    [From "Zhun Zheng", same below]
    Treats uterine bleeding that does not stop.
    Four ounces of Xiang Fu, stir-fried; one ounce and two qian of Dang Gui tail; one ounce of Wu Ling Zhi, stir-fried.
    Grind them into a fine powder, take five qian, mix with vinegar broth, take on an empty stomach for immediate effect.

    Su Sha San
    Treats uterine bleeding.
    Su Sha Ren in any amount, stir-fried on a new tile until fragrant.
    Grind into a fine powder, mix with rice drink; take three qian.

    Liang Xue Di Huang Decoction
    Treats uterine bleeding that does not stop; kidney water is yin-deficient and cannot stabilize the fire of the Bao Luo, hence the blood flows out and bleeds.
    Five fen each of fresh Di Huang and Dang Gui tail; two fen each of Huang Lian, Huang Bo, Zhi Mu, Gao Ben, Chuan Xiong, Sheng Ma; a little red flower; one fen each of Chai Hu, Fang Feng, Qiang Huo, Huang Qin, Xi Xin, Jing Jie Su, Man Jing Zi, and roasted Gan Cao.
    Mince the above, steep in three bowls of water, reduce to one bowl, strain, and take warm on an empty stomach.[66p]
    Danggui Shaoyao Decoction: For women with continuous menstrual bleeding, the blood is bright red. Occurring in July during the heat, initially due to overexertion, with weak spleen and stomach, shortness of breath, persistent sweating, body heat and confusion, aversion to food, loss of appetite, fatigue, weak limbs, and diarrhea. Later, due to insufficient heart energy, the bleeding continues. They feel the energy descending, and the vital energy is reversed upwards, leading to an inability to speak; this is not laziness to speak but a lack of strength to do so. This medicine addresses it.

    Ingredients: 1.5 liang Huangqi; 5 qian each of Baizhu, Cangzhu (washed to remove skin), Guishen (Angelica root), Baishao (white peony), and Gancao (licorice, honey-fried); 3 fen each of Shengdi (raw rehmannia) and Chao (fried) Zhi; 5 fen each of Shudi (cooked rehmannia) and Chenpi (dried tangerine peel without white); 2 fen Chaihu.

    These ten ingredients are ground into coarse powder, made into two doses, boiled in water, the residue discarded, and taken hot on an empty stomach, gradually reducing thereafter. The next day, all symptoms will dissipate, and they will feel joyful about food. Why is this? Because the energy has flowed and they can smell the aroma of food.

    Bai Zi Ren Decoction: Used for women excessively worried, injuring the heart, unable to store blood, leading to continuous bleeding during menstruation.

    Ingredients: 1.5 qian each of Chao Baiziren (fried biota seed), Chao Xiangfu (fried nutgrass), and Lurong (antler, roasted and soaked in wine); 1.5 qian each of Xiongfu (rhizome of ligusticum), and Chao (fried) Aijiao (donkey-hide gelatin); 2 qian Xuduan (dipsacus root); 0.5 qian of honey-fried Gan cao.

    These ingredients make one dose, with two zhong of water, five slices of fresh ginger, boiled down to one zhong, and taken on an empty stomach.[67p]
    Yiwai Shengyang Decoction: Treats excessive uterine bleeding; use to promote blood and facilitate digestion.

    Ingredients:

    • Astragalus and Scutellaria, each 6 grams
    • Ginseng and toasted Shenqu, each 1.5 grams
    • Cimicifuga and Bupleurum, each 2.5 grams
    • Atractylodes 9 grams
    • Angelica, wine-soaked, and honey-fried Licorice, and dried tangerine peel, each 3 grams

    Preparation: Grind into a coarse powder. Take 9-15 grams per dose; if eating, add more; if not eating, reduce by 9 grams. Do not exceed dosages. For abdominal pain, add 0.6 grams of white peony; if thirsty, add 0.6 grams of Kudzu; if coughing, omit ginseng. Another method uses raw Rehmannia. If this does not stop the bleeding, take the following formula.

    Chaihu Tiaojing Decoction: Treats prolonged menstruation, fresh bleeding, tense neck muscles, headaches, severe back pain, and loss of appetite.

    Ingredients:

    • Qianghuo, Duhuo, dried root, Cimicifuga, each 2.5 grams
    • Atractylodes 3 grams
    • Bupleurum root 2.5 grams
    • Kudzu, Angelica, honey-fried Licorice, each 1 gram
    • A pinch of safflower

    Preparation: Grind, prepare one dose, boil in water, strain, warm it slightly, take on an empty stomach to induce a light sweat which will stop the symptoms.[68p]
    Regulating Menstruation and Ascending Yang to Eliminate Dampness Decoction (also known as Ascending Yang to Eliminate Dampness Decoction). This treats women with abnormal vaginal discharge of dark blood, irregular menstruation, or sudden excessive bleeding that does not stop, excessive discharge of watery substance, lethargy, a tendency to lie down, unresponsive limbs, fatigue and weakness, shortness of breath, a feeling of urgency, with a pulse that is slow and tight, and a large flood pulse when pressed beneath the middle finger. After taking this medicine and recovering, it is advisable to replenish qi and harmonize the blood. Ingredients include:

    • Chai Hu 1 qian
    • Fang Feng 1 qian
    • Honey-fried Gan Cao 1 qian
    • Shao Ben 1 qian
    • Sheng Ma 1 qian
    • Qiang Huo 1.5 qian
    • Cang Zhu 1.5 qian
    • Huang Qi 1.5 qian
    • Man Jing Zi 0.7 qian
    • Du Huo 0.5 qian
    • Dang Gui soaked in wine 0.5 qian

    Crush the above ingredients, add 5 large cups of water, and boil down to 1 large cup. Strain and drink it warm, on an empty stomach. After a while, have a light breakfast to suppress it; one dose is sufficient.

    Modified Four Substance Decoction treats young girls when the menstrual flow has reached age 14 but may sometimes arrive late, or when there is sudden excessive bleeding that does not stop, resembling a flood and leakage, causing excessive blood loss and various other symptoms. It is suitable for such situations. Ingredients include:

    • Chuan Xiong 1 liang
    • Shu Di Huang 1 liang
    • Dang Gui, roasted with wine 1 liang
    • Bai Shao 1 liang
    • Xiang Fu, stir-fried 1.5 liang

    Crush the above ingredients, each time take 4 qian, with 1.5 cups of water and 5 slices of fresh ginger, boil down to 70%, strain, and drink warm before meals. If the blood appears bright and does not stop, remove Shu Di Huang and add Sheng Di Huang to decoct.[69p]
    Dihuang Pill treats the deficiency of the three yin of the feet, with menstruation lasting several days and possibly accompanied by discharge without conception. Ingredients: prepared Rehmannia, Schisandra fruit, and woody nightshade seeds, each one tael; lightly fried white peony root, and red ochre, each one tael; dried ginger, and lightly fried silkworms, and magnolia bark processed with ginger, each three qian. Grind the above into a powder, form into honey pills the size of tung seeds. Take fifty pills at a time with warm wine on an empty stomach, three times a day.

    Xiaojizhong Decoction treats persistent uterine bleeding with a clear, watery color, which becomes worse with warmth, indicating an injury to yin and causing blood flow. Pulse may be rapid and thin (indicating disharmony) or forceful (indicating excess). Ingredients: juice from ground Xiaojizhong stems and leaves, and juice from ground raw Rehmannia, each one cup; half a tael of Atractylodes; grind these three ingredients, add one cup of water, and simmer until reduced by half, then strain and take warm.

    Qiongzhong Wine treats continuous bleeding day and night, difficult to treat by physicians. Ingredients: one tael of Qiongzhong; one cup of raw Rehmannia juice. Add five cups of wine, boil one cup of Qiongzhong to remove residue, add Rehmannia juice, simmer for two or three boils, and divide into three doses.

    Miraculous Four Substance Decoction treats long-standing blood loss due to heat. Ingredients: whole Angelica, white peony, Chuanxiong, and prepared Rehmannia, washed and roasted; mugwort leaves; stir-fried Ejiao pearl powder; and Huangqin, removing the black parts, each half a tael. Grind and break these ingredients; take four qian with one and a half cups of water, adding five slices of ginger, simmer until reduced to seven portions, and take warm on an empty stomach.[70p]
    Jinhua San
    For treating blood stagnation with heat, where bleeding does not stop, and warm medicine is ineffective.
    Ingredients:

    • Yanhusuo 1 liang
    • Qumaisui 1 liang
    • Danggui 1 liang
    • Ganjiang 1 liang
    • Danpi 1 liang
    • Shigao 2 liang
    • Guixin 7.5 qian
    • Weilingxian 7.5 qian
    • Puhuang 0.5 liang
      Prepare as a fine powder. For each dose, take 3 qian, mix with 1.5 cups of water, and consume warm on an empty stomach, twice a day.

    Another formula for treating unceasing blood loss in the lower body with abdominal pain:

    • Shaoyao 1 liang, slightly roasted
    • Boliye 6 pieces, lightly roasted

    Use 1 sheng of water, boil to gather 6 cups, add 5 he of wine, simmer to obtain 7 cups. Take on an empty stomach, split into two doses. A related method involves finely grinding 2 qian mixed with wine. Another approach includes equal parts of deer antler waist, mixed with wine, to treat white discharge and lower abdominal pain.

    Lixiao San (Dongyuan)
    For treating blood collapse.
    Ingredients:

    • Danggui 1 liang
    • Lianhua rui 1 liang
    • Baimianzi 1 liang
    • Honghua 1 liang

    Grind together, package with salt mud, and fire calcine to make a fine powder, adding a little musk. Each dose is 2 qian, mixed with warm wine and consumed on an empty stomach.

    Muli San (from "Zhunjing" as below)
    For treating unceasing menstrual flow, unresponsive to many medicines.
    Ingredients:
    Finely powdered Muli, fire calcined, mixed with vinegar to form pills, then re-calcined to bright red, cooled and re-ground to eliminate fire toxins.
    Combine with vinegar and Ai powder, reduce to a paste, forming pills the size of a walnut. For each dose, take 50 pills with vinegar and Ai decoction.[71p]
    Bai Huang San: Treats continuous menstrual bleeding.
    Ingredients: 1.5 taels of Huang Qin, 1 tael each of cypress leaves and catkin, 2 taels of Fu Long Gan.
    Crush the ingredients, add 2 liters of water, boil down to 800 ml, and divide into two doses.

    Ding Xiang Jiao Ai Tang: Treats excessive menstrual bleeding. It is due to insufficient heart qi, overexertion, and irregular diet leading to insufficient blood flow. The pulse at both wrists is tight and forceful, but weak upon pressure; one feels cold in the lower abdomen, seeking thick clothing for warmth. The white discharge resembles water leaking from a roof, with occasional fresh blood but not much. The right wrist pulse is occasionally vigorous; frequent leaking indicates a tense pulse due to excess cold, while a vigorous pulse indicates less heat. Overall, the tight pulse reflects excessive cold water from the north, while the vigorous pulse is associated with the fire in the Ming Men. The dampness is prevalent, while dryness is minimal, resembling leaking roof water.

    Ingredients: 0.4 taels of Chuan Xiong and Ding Xiang, 0.3 taels of Shu Di Huang and Bai Shao, 0.6 taels of fried Ejiao, 1.2 taels of Dang Gui’s body, and 1 tael of fresh Ai Ye.
    Grind the above into a fine powder, prepare one dose, add 2 cups of water, boil until it has boiled five times, strain, and add into the mixture. Cook again until reduced to 1 cup, take on an empty stomach after the previous meal is digested, and take while hot. Repeat three times for effect.

    Another formula to treat blood flooding.[72p]
    Dried mugwort the size of a chicken egg; half a tael of ejiao; one dime of dried ginger. Grind these into a coarse powder. Use five cups of water, first simmer the mugwort and ginger until reduced to two and a half cups, then add the ejiao to dissolve. Divide into two doses and take on an empty stomach.

    Qiong-Yuan Decoction, also known as Qiong-Yuan Warming Middle Decoction. It treats prolonged menstrual discharge with continuous bleeding, as well as conditions caused by wind, deficiency of cold, exhaustion of the Chong and Ren vessels, irregular menstruation, sudden heavy bleeding, heavy waist and urgent abdominal pain, persistent dripping; also for excessive postpartum blood loss, weakness, and abdominal pain; or during pregnancy when there is instability of the fetus with ongoing bleeding for several days, frequent urination, fatigue, dizziness, dim vision, and lack of appetite.

    The ingredients include: two taels each of Qiong-Yuan, roasted astragalus, white peony root, dried rehmannia, evodia, and licorice; and one tael each of angelica and dried ginger. Grind these and use one斗 of water to boil down to three sheng, divided into three doses. If this occurs after menstruation with persistent red and white discharge, remove dried rehmannia and evodia, adding two taels each of Eucommia and ginseng.[73p]
    Duansha Tang treats weakness of the Chong and Ren channels, abnormal uterine bleeding, and irregularities in the meridians. Whenever the menstrual period approaches, pain occurs first in the navel, abdomen, lower back, and legs, gradually leading to reduced appetite, weakness in the limbs, and various discharge-related illnesses. It can cure all of these conditions.

    Ingredients:

    • Ginseng (skinned) - 1 tael
    • Cooked Rehmannia (washed and roasted) - 1 tael
    • Mugwort leaves - 1 tael (vinegar-fried)
    • Fish bone ash - 2 taels
    • Ligusticum wallichii (washed with wine) - 2 taels
    • Dried ginger (processed) - ½ tael
    • Sichuan lovage - 7 qian
    • Donkey-hide gelatin and clam powder fried into beads - 7.5 qian

    Preparation: Grind the above ingredients, and take 5 qian of the mixture with 1.5 sheng of water, simmer until reduced to 7 parts, then strain and take warm before meals.

    Another formula to treat abnormal bleeding:

    • Siwu Decoction - 1 tael
    • Ginseng - 2 qian
    • Evodia - 1 qian

    Preparation: Grind the ingredients finely, take ½ tael each time, decoct with ginger and jujube, and consume before meals. After five or six doses, chills, fevers, and abdominal pain will all subside. If bleeding does not stop, continue with the following Fu Zi pills.

    Fu Zi Pills to treat abnormal bleeding:

    • Processed Aconite - ½ tael
    • Equisetum - ½ tael
    • Calcined dragon bone - ½ tael
    • Calcined red resin - ½ tael
    • Sichuan lovage - 1 tael
    • Angelica sinensis - 1 tael

    Preparation: Grind to a fine powder, mix with vinegar to form pills the size of a wuzi (a small nut). Take 50 to 60 pills each time, consuming with rice drink before meals.

    Shen Ying San treats uncontrolled uterine bleeding.[74p]
    Gui Xin: The heart of cassia does not adhere to quantity; when fried to a charred state, it retains its properties. Take the powder as needed, with one or two qian mixed in rice wine for consumption.

    Deer Antler Pill: For treating excessive postmenstrual bleeding, with dark and stagnant color, especially when there is continuous bleeding and faint breathing, accompanied by extreme cold in the navel and abdomen, leading to heavy sweating, and a pulse that is weak and small. This occurs when the channels are weak and cold wind invades, preventing the qi from being sealed properly; it is beneficial to moxibust at the Guan Yuan point. Ingredients: Dehaired and lightly roasted deer antler, chi stone resin, Yu Yu grain, each one liang; half liang of mugwort leaves, cypress leaves, and prepared aconite; two liang each of cooked rehmannia, angelica, and dried rehmannia. Grind into a fine powder, mix with a little wine paste, and form into walnut-sized pills. Take thirty pills every time with warm wine on an empty stomach. A version with honey pills is also acceptable; another version does not include mugwort leaves.

    Chi Long Dan: For treating continuous bleeding from the womb.

    Ingredients: Yu Yu grain fried, fish bone, deer antler, dragon bone, calcined stone swallow, ejiao (donkey-hide gelatin), angelica, and dried ginger, each in equal parts. Grind into a powder, mix with wine vinegar paste, and form into pills. Take fifty pills with warm wine.

    Zi Jin San: For treating excessive menstrual flow, continuous bleeding and leucorrhea, persistent dripping, heavy pain in the waist and abdomen, and various five-colored ailments. Ingredients: Yu Yu grain calcined with red vinegar and concentrated seven times, finely sifted with water, three liang; one liang each of white peony root, sinochilli, cooked rehmannia, aconite, angelica, chi stone resin, and dragon bone separately calcined; half liang each of dried ginger and roasted cinnamon. Grind into a fine powder and take two qian, adding a little musk and mixing with rice wine taken on an empty stomach.[75p]
    Bai Zhi Warming the Palace Pill
    This formula calms the blood, treats uterine weakness, addresses wind and cold stagnation, and resolves issues with failing pregnancy and multiple miscarriages. It is also effective for discharges that are red or white, foul-smelling, or that come in various colors, along with symptoms of weakness, low energy, fullness and pain in the chest and abdomen, heart palpitations, night sweats, and excessive menstrual bleeding.

    Ingredients:
    Yuyu Lou formula – 1 liang
    Dry ginger, roasted
    Peony
    Sichuan pepper, prepared
    Bai zhi
    Donkey-hide gelatin, fried with clam powder
    Mugwort, prepared
    Chuanxiong, each 7.5 qian

    Grind these into a fine powder, mix with honey to form pills the size of a walnut. Take 40 pills with rice drink, warm wine, or vinegar soup as needed.

    Amber Powder
    Also known as Black Hat Powder. It treats severe, unchecked bleeding.

    Ingredients:
    Black hat (referring to black silk headscarves), red peony, fragrant ageratum, dry lotus leaves, men's hair washed with soap pod water, angelica, burnt palm, all in equal parts.

    Grind all except the palm into coarse pieces on a new tile and then into black ash. Combine into a fine powder. Take 5 qian on an empty stomach with children’s urine, repeating after walking ten miles, stopping after 7 to 8 doses. If post-partum blood loss persists, add rice vinegar, Beijing ink, and a small amount of musk.[76p]
    Jingjie San: For treating continuous bleeding in women.
    Jingjie is best with sesame oil for lighting lamps; the more, the darker the wick burns. Grind fine, take three qian mixed with child’s urine.

    Kaiyu Siwu Decoction: For treating bleeding caused by the heart's energy, thus it’s important to seize the momentum; wealth precedes poverty in all matters.
    Stir-fried Xiangfu rice, one qian each of: Danggui, white peony root (stir-fried in wine), cooked Rehmannia, and Bai Zhu. Each five qian of: Chuanxiong, Huangqi, and fried Puhuang, Dihu, and Ren Shen. Three fen of Shengma. Grind, form into one packet, and boil for consumption.

    Wujin San: For relentless blood flow.
    Palm ashes, one liang; dragon bone, calcined, two qian. Grind finely and mix; take two qian with good wine on an empty stomach; two doses will promptly stop the bleeding.
    Another prescription for unending blood flow, forming pieces of blood like broken chicken liver.
    Chuanxiong twelve fen, Ejiao, and Qingzhuzhu, each eight fen; Xuduan, Dihu, and Xiaojizigen, each three fen; Danggui six fen; Shengdihuang and Fulonggan, each eleven fen. Use nine sheng of water, boil down to three sheng, strain, and divide into three doses.

    Wubi San: For treating women's blood flow.
    One liang of silkworm sand, stir-fried; half a liang of Fulonggan. Grind together to a powder, mix with warm wine, take two to three qian on an empty stomach; this is the dosage. Another formula includes one liang of Ejiao.[77p]
    Fulong Gan San treats injuries to qi and blood, as well as deficiencies in the Chong and Ren meridians. The classics state: when there is an unexpected sudden onset of bleeding, it may appear like bean juice, or as blood clots, sometimes mixed with five colors, or a combination of red and white. The abdomen feels cold and painful, and has not ceased for a long time, leading to a person appearing yellow and emaciated with a dry mouth, reduced appetite, and weakness in the limbs with restlessness and palpitations.

    Ingredients:

    • Fulong Gan: 1 liang
    • Chi Shi Zhi: 1 liang
    • Xiong Huang: 3 liang
    • Gan Cao: 0.5 liang
    • Rou Gui: 0.5 liang
    • Shu Di Huang: 2 liang
    • Ai Ye (slightly stir-fried): 2 liang
    • Dang Gui: 0.75 liang
    • Gan Jiang: 0.75 liang
    • Mai Men Dong (heart removed): 1.5 liang

    The above ingredients are ground into a coarse powder. Each dose is 4 qian, along with one Jujube and water, then decocted for consumption.

    Diyu San treats continuous bleeding in women during menstruation.

    Ingredients:

    • Diyu (ground)
    • Pu Huang
    • Bai Shao Yao
    • Bai Fu Ling
    • Bai Ye (slightly stir-fried)
    • Xie Zhua (slightly stir-fried)
    • Shu Di Huang
    • Lu Jiao Jiao (crushed and stir-fried until yellow)
    • Lou Lu: 1 liang each
    • Xiong Huang
    • Dang Gui (ground and stir-fried): 0.75 liang each
    • Fulong Gan: 1.5 liang
    • Gan Jiang (prepared)
    • Gui Xin
    • Gan Cao (ground and roasted red): 0.5 liang each

    The above ingredients are ground into a powder. Each dose is 3 qian, mixed with one medium cup of water, adding 1 fen of Bamboo Reeds, decocted down to 7 parts. Strain and take warm before meals.

    The above four formulas all use Fulong Gan as it is effective in removing dampness. However, the first two formulas eliminate damp-heat, while the latter two dispel cold dampness. It is noted that Long Gan is a sacred medicine for removing dampness and stopping bleeding. The ancients treated bleeding with Xuanfu Hua and Ban Xia, intending to stop bleeding by addressing phlegm-damp in the diaphragm, reflecting the same principle.[78p]
    Deer Antler Powder
    For treating continuous bleeding during menstruation, particularly when accompanied by weakness and emaciation.
    Two liang of deer antler, fur removed, lightly roasted until slightly yellow.
    White dragon bone, cooked rehmannia, soft-shelled turtle shell roasted until yellow, fried fish bone roasted until yellow, white peony root, white resin, and Dipsacus asarum, each one liang;
    one and a half liang of Cistanche, soaked in wine overnight, skin scraped off and dried.
    Grind the above into a fine powder; take two qian before meals, mixed with porridge or drink.

    Cypress Leaf Powder
    For treating women's continuous bleeding during menstruation, regardless of the duration, gradually leading to pallor, weakness in the limbs, abdominal pain, and loss of appetite.
    One and a half liang each of cypress leaves, Dipsacus, Ligusticum, fresh rehmannia, angelica, tortoise shell, and soft-shelled turtle shell;
    two and a half liang of Yu Yu Liang, one liang each of donkey-hide gelatin, oyster shell, ground earth, red resin, mugwort leaves, and deer antler.
    Grind the above into a fine powder; take two qian before meals, mixed with porridge or drink.
    Another formula includes Salvia miltiorrhiza, add deer antler, prepare into pills the size of a jujube, take thirty to forty pills on an empty stomach, using warm wine or vinegar soup to wash down.[79p]
    Bu Gong Wan (Supplementing the Uterus Pill)
    Treats women suffering from various deficiencies, prolonged infertility, heat in the bones, emaciation, and abnormal vaginal discharge.
    Ingredients:

    • Bai Wei (White Microcosm)
    • Mu Li (Oyster Shell)
    • Bai Shao (White Peony)
    • Lu Jiao Shuang (Deer Antler Velvet)
    • Shan Yao (Chinese Yam)
    • Fu Ling (Poria)
    • Bai Zhu (White Atractylodes)
    • Liao Zhai (Fishbone)
    • Bai Zhi (Angelica)
      All in equal parts.
      Grind the above into a fine powder, mix with water to form pills the size of walnuts. Take fifty pills each time with rice drink on an empty stomach.

    Zhen Gong Wan (Calming the Uterus Pill)
    Treats women with persistent bleeding, which may present as five colors, or alternately red and white, sometimes resembling bean juice, or like pig liver, or with dark blood clots, belly swelling and pain, dizziness, prolonged conditions leading to yellowing, emaciation, dry mouth, and restlessness without appetite.
    Ingredients:

    • Dai Zhe Shi (Hematite) fire calcined, vinegar tempered
    • Zi Shi Qing (Amethyst)
    • Yu Yu Liang (Yue Yu Grain) prepared as above
    • Xiang Fu (Cyperus) boiled with vinegar, each two taels
    • Yang Qi Shi (Yin Stone) fired, finely ground
    • Pu Huang (Cattail Pollen) stir-fried
    • Lu Rung (Deer Velvet) de-furred, steamed and baked with vinegar
    • A Jiao (Donkey-hide Gelatin), crushed with clam powder, stir-fried into beads
    • Fu Shen (Poria Spirit)
    • Dang Gui (Angelica Sinensis) steeped in wine
    • Xiong Dan (Chuanxiong) each one tael,
    • Xue Jie (Dragon's Blood) half a tael, ground
      Grind the above into a fine powder, use mugwort to brew vinegar juice, cook glutinous rice flour into a paste to form pills the size of walnuts. Take seventy pills each time with rice drink on an empty stomach.[80p]
      Mei Yin Zi treats women's blood collapse.
      Salted white plum ashes retain their nature.
      The right ingredients are ground, mixed in hollow rice drink to take.
      Another formula treats persistent bleeding, with intermittent episodes.
      One ounce of calcined alum juice, one penny of myrrh, five fen each of refractory sand and yellow lead; these should be melted together into juice, the remaining fine powders mixed in, and stirred uniformly until formed into pills the size of a bullet. Each time, use one pill wrapped in new cotton, inserted into the vagina, to achieve immediate effect.
      Ten Ash Pills treat continuous bleeding with a heavy flow.
      Silk, yellow silk, horsehair, mugwort leaves, lotus nodes, lotus flower centers, oilgrass, red pine bark, palm, and cattail pollen, all calcined into ashes, in equal parts. Grind these well, use vinegar to cook glutinous rice paste, and mix into pills the size of a walnut. Take seventy pills initially, increasing to one hundred, accompanied by hollow rice drink.
      Ten Ash Powder treats unending lower bleeding.
      Silk fabric, horsetail, palm, cypress leaves, carp scales, crucian carp scales, mugwort leaves, shellac, bloodwort, and angelica, all calcined to retain their nature, in equal parts, with a little musk. Grind these well, taking two qian each time, mixed with warm empty wine.
      Another formula treats blood collapse with repeated effects.[81p]
      Angelica, white peony, dried ginger, and palm, all in equal parts.
      Each ingredient should be calcined to retain its properties, ground into a fine powder, mixed with vinegar, and stirred leftward with a red chopstick for forty-nine turns. Take before meals.

    Fragrant Alum Powder treats vaginal bleeding.
    The amount of Cyperus rotundus is not fixed, it should be very sour, soaked overnight in vinegar, then fried until charred to form ash. For every two taels of this, add two qian of powdered alum, mix with rice wine to take on an empty stomach; it is effective. A decoction of lotus leaves is particularly excellent.

    Holy Powder treats severe bleeding.
    For one tael each of palm and black plum meat, and one and a half taels of dried ginger, all should be calcined. Grind to a fine powder, take three qian each time, mixed with black plum wine, on an empty stomach before meals. If the condition has persisted for long, three doses will suffice.

    Single formula:

    In cases of bleeding with green, yellow, red, or white, causing infertility: one qian of good ink, taken with water, twice a day.
    Another formula: for sudden bleeding, grind good ink with vinegar and take it.
    Another formula: two qian of hundred herb frost, mixed with dog gall juice, divided into two doses, taken with Angelica wine.
    Another formula: two taels of blue alum, one qian of lightweight powder, made into pills the size of walnuts; take thirty to forty pills after meals, with ginger soup.[82p]
    Red and white vaginal bleeding: One can use stone fire to refine and take as a remedy, which is good.

    For women's blood loss: Take daizhe stone, calcine it with fire, grind it into powder seven times, and take with white rice vinegar. Another remedy: Use half a tael of guanzhong, boil it in wine, and take it immediately to stop the bleeding.

    For persistent red and white vaginal bleeding in women, which makes one pale and thin: Take three taels of diyu, one liter of rice vinegar, boil it for a while, strain it, and take one dose slightly warm before meals.

    For women’s blood loss: Grind sanqi into powder, mix with a little diluted white wine, and take three doses to recover. If necessary, add five fen and use it in the Four Substances Decoction as well. Another remedy: Grind huangqin into fine powder, take one qian for each dose with strong liquor, using a rod to burn it red and mixing it into the wine. Xu Xueshi says: In cases of bleeding, one often uses hemostatic and blood-replenishing herbs. This formula treats the yang overpowering the yin, as if the heat of heaven warms the earth, causing the menstrual flow to overflow.

    Another remedy: Mix jin si cao, hai tong pi, sha ren, hua jiao, silkworm cast-off paper, and old silk ash in equal parts, grind into powder, boil in wine, and take on an empty stomach.[83p]
    A woman experiences excessive bleeding and cannot conceive: boil Angelica and drink the juice.

    In cases of continuous bleeding with blood loss day and night: grind one ounce of Chuanxiong into powder, mix with a large cup of clear wine, decoct to obtain five portions, and take it slowly.

    Another prescription: mix equal parts of Cyperus rotundus and Red Peony, grind into powder, take two qian each time with a pinch of salt, prepared with one cup of water, decoct down to seven portions, and take warm, twice daily; effects can be seen after ten doses.

    Another prescription: fry and finely grind Yizhi fruit, mix with rice wine and salt, take one qian.

    For blood loss with abundant bleeding or mixed colors: fry and roast Cyperus rotundus until charred, grind it into powder, take two qian with extremely hot wine for immediate relief; for severe faintness, take three qian with rice wine. It can also be mixed with wood ash.

    For severe bleeding that threatens life: take one liter of boiled chicken sowthistle juice.[84p]
    Excessive bleeding: Prepare summer withered grass into a powder, and take a small amount mixed with rice wine. Another prescription: The roots of large and small thistles, one liter, and one dou of wine, soak for five nights. It can be consumed as a wine decoction or fresh juice warmed and taken. Another prescription: Squeeze half a liter of juice from the large thistle root, which will stop the bleeding immediately.

    Another prescription: Four taels of wood raspberry root, one bowl of wine, decoct for seven parts, and take it warm on an empty stomach. For bleeding and dysentery, occurring dozens of times both day and night, with a wish for death: Take one he of hanging hook root, rose root, persimmon root, and bramble root, shred and place in a pot, submerge with water to four or five inches, boil down to one-third, strain for the juice, then boil until it becomes pills the size of walnuts. Take ten pills with warm wine, three times daily.

    For leaking during bleeding: Use stone reed ground into a powder, take three qian with warm wine for excellent effect. For women’s bleeding: Steam fresh bloodwort and mix with oil, salt, and ginger for consumption, followed by one or two cups of wine; or dry it into powder and take one or two qian mixed with ginger wine, which will stop the bleeding immediately. Found growing in brick crevices, with little on the ground. Another prescription: Grind fine tea of stone flower into powder, take one spoonful of old lacquer dish burned to retain its essence, place in a bowl of wine, and boil in water until bubbling. Then add the powdered medicine, let it steep overnight, and re-boil in the morning, then warm and take.[85p]
    For bleeding that does not stop: use the root of hemp boiled in water as a remedy.
    For bleeding with red and white discharge: take a handful of grave head return grass, half a cup of wine and water, half a bowl of boy's urine, one pinch of safflower, decoct until reduced to seven parts, and take it warm at bedtime; for those recently affected, take once; if long-standing, take three doses to cure, with effects like magic.
    For uncontrolled hemorrhage: dry the white sophora flower and grind it into powder, taking two qian each time, stir in a little salt and drink with hollow roasted rice decoction; this is effective. Another formula: use old steamed cakes burned to retain their essence, taking three qian with rice drink. Another formula involves heating an iron vessel red hot and soaking it in wine, which is called "thunder wine." Another formula: mash one tael of lake chicken leg roots, mix with two cups of wine, and decoct to one cup to drink.
    For bleeding wounds: use the juice of the milk liquid vegetable, mix with wine and boil, drink one cup on an empty stomach.
    For leaking discharge: take half a jin of wood ear mushrooms, fry until smoke appears and grind into powder, each time take two qian and one fen, along with three fen of hair, totaling two qian and four fen; consume with good wine to induce sweating. Another formula: grind black mulberry ears fried to powder and take with wine, about the size of a thumb, three times a day for effect.[86p]
    Blood cannot stop gushing: equal parts of burnt old loofah and palm ash, taken with salt wine or salted soup.

    For bleeding without regard to the length of time: use burnt locust beans ground to powder, take the size of a small spoon with warm wine.

    If the bleeding does not stop and other remedies are ineffective: use burnt bitter almonds mixed with yellow peels, ground to powder, take three qian with hot empty wine.

    For continuous blood loss: use seven pieces of dried black plum, ground to powder, take with rice drink three times a day.

    For persistent leakage during bleeding: finely grind burnt walnut, take the size of a small spoon with wine three times a day. Another remedy: use fifteen pieces of walnut meat, burn to ashes, grind and take with warm wine on an empty stomach for effective results.

    For bleeding accompanied by discharge: fry and finely grind peppercorn, take one qian with warm wine.

    For unyielding blood gushing, irrespective of temperature: use burnt lotus seed pods and fragrant schizope with equal proportions of ash, take two qian with rice drink. Another remedy: burn half a liang of lotus leaves, combine with one liang each of cattail pollen and yellow skullcap, ground together, and take three qian with empty wine.

    Another remedy: use one year-old water chestnut, burn to ashes and grind, take with wine.[87p]

    • For women with persistent bleeding: burn and grind Sophora flower, take two to three qian mixed with warm wine before meals.
    • For uncontrollable uterine bleeding: three liang of Sophora flower, two liang of Huangqin, powdered, take half a liang with a cup of wine; heat a bronze weight until red and add it to the wine before consuming, avoid certain foods.
    • For lower body bleeding: one liang of Sophora flower, five qian of palm ash, one qian of salt, decoct and reduce to half for consumption.
    • For women with continuous bleeding day and night: two liang of clove, two sheng of wine, decoct to one sheng, divide into doses.
    • For bleeding with red and white discharge, regardless of distance: take ash from burned Sophora branches and consume with wine before meals, three times a day.
    • For bleeding with discharge: mix and smoke with myrobalan and wax, and use the decoction to wash.
    • For women's uterine bleeding: half a jin of Tang di peel (also known as Fu yi), four liang of peony root, one liang each of sesame and calcined oyster, use one liang each, with two zhong of wine, decoct to one zhong and take before meals.
    • Another method: take mulberry silk powder and consume three to five qian with wine.[88p]
    • For bleeding during a miscarriage: regularly roast pig kidneys and consume them.
    • For women with excessive menstrual bleeding: wash and clean loose hair, then dry it and grind it to powder. Take one qian with warm wine.
    • For red and white bleeding: roast fish glue for three feet until yellow, grind into powder, mix with chicken egg pancake, and consume with good wine. Another prescription: use ash from old worn-out cattail mats, take two qian with wine.
    • For relentless hemorrhage: mix one qian each of lacquer ash and palm fiber ash, brew with cypress leaves and take as soup.
    • For women with excessive bleeding: Zhen Quan suggests grinding turtle shell and vinegar into powder and taking one inch-sized dose with clear wine twice daily. Another prescription: mix dried ginger, turtle shell, and haritaki bark into powder, form into pills, and take thirty pills on an empty stomach, twice a day. Yet another prescription: mix good cotton and women's hair, burn them to retain their essence, then mix with herbal frost in equal parts, take three qian each time with warm wine; or add ash from mulberry tree. Another prescription: use one jin of old cotton fluff free from dust, one jin of new silk, ten old lotus rooms, and one old reed stem, all burned to retain their essence, take one qian each with hot wine three times a day, without exceeding five days for recovery. Another prescription: take ash from old sow dung and consume two qian with wine.[89p]
      For persistent bleeding with a mix of red and white, accompanied by weakness and exhaustion: grind three taels each of turtle shell and oyster into a powder, take it with a teaspoon of wine three times a day.

    Another prescription: take one and a half taels of mulberry leaves, eighteen grains of deer antler, and five liters of vinegar; steep and roast until dry, then soak again until the vinegar is gone, treating it before straining and taking a teaspoon three times a day.

    Another prescription: equal parts of earth smoke and anemarrhena, each cut to an inch long, chew and mix with one liter of vinegar, then heat in an east-facing stove until concentrated, discard the residue and take the liquid.

    For women with continuous menstrual bleeding: grind clematis flower into a powder, take two qian with wine, followed by taking "Four Substances Decoction."

    Another prescription: burn palm bark until ash remains, consume three qian with light wine.

    Another prescription: burn palm bark until ash remains and mix equally with burnt alum, consume three qian with light wine.

    Another prescription: boil hemp seed roots and take the resulting liquid for effect.[90p]

    • In cases of excessive bleeding with five colors, which causes infertility: Take the ends of bee combs, pinch with three fingers, and take it with warm wine; it is very effective. 【Zhang Wenzhong's Formula】

    • For unceasing blood flow during collapse: Use white silkworms and white fish, ground into powder in equal parts, and take with well water twice daily. 【"Thousand Gold"】

    • For women's blood collapse: Drink the juice of boiled silkworm cocoons.

    • For continuous bleeding during collapse: Fry one sheet of old silkworm paper until charred, and equally mix it with roasted locust seeds, grind into powder, and take with wine; it will take effect immediately. Another formula: Use four taels of silkworm sand, fry it until half yellow, add one pot of wine without ash, boil it and remove the sand; take one cup warm each time to cure. 【"Materia Medica"】

    • For unceasing blood leakage: Fry leeches into powder, take one qian with wine, twice a day, and the bad blood will disappear and cure. 【"Thousand Gold"】

    • In cases of mixed red and white collapse: Fry an old gourd and lotus near the flame to retain their nature, grind into equal parts powder, take two qian with hot water, and stop after three doses if sweating occurs; for severe cases, stop after five doses. It is best to avoid sexual activity and foods that are cold. 【"Marine Herbal Formulas"】

    • Acupuncture

    The "Classic of Acupuncture" states: For abdominal pain during collapse, needling the Qiaokong point is indicated. For women with blood leakage that feels blocked and causes reverse Qi and distension, the Blood Sea point is indicated. For women with blood leakage, the Taichong point is indicated.[91p]
    A woman experiencing menstrual bleeding with abdominal distension and difficulty in breathing, and yellow urine, should be treated at Yin Gu. The "Thousand Golden Prescriptions" states: if there is menstrual bleeding with abdominal distension as if blocked, accompanied by cold and heat in the body, with swelling on one side of the abdomen, puncture Yin Gu to four-tenths and moxibustion three times. The point is located beneath the large tendon behind the inner knee bone, above the small tendon, and can be accessed by bending the knee.

    If bleeding leads to blockage, causing reversed qi and distension, puncture Xue Hai to five-tenths and moxibustion five times. This point is located two inches below the inner border of the kneecap at the edge of the white flesh.

    For women experiencing menstrual issues or excessive bleeding, puncture Ran Gu to three-tenths and moxibustion three times. This point is located in the depression under the prominent bone in front of the inner ankle.

    For women with excessive bleeding, painful pressure in the chest, and delayed menstruation, the points Shui Yuan and Zhao Hai should be treated.

    If a woman has blood flowing from the back of the womb, it should not be forcibly stopped; moxibustion should be applied three inches from either side of Guan Yuan.

    For women with irregular menstruation manifesting as red or white bleeding, apply moxibustion thirty times at Jiao Xin, located five inches above the inner ankle.

    For women with red or white bleeding, apply moxibustion fifty times at the point Tai Yin, located three inches above the inner ankle on the Tai Yin channel, known as San Yin Jiao.

    For women with red or white bleeding and soreness in the limbs, moxibustion should be applied thirty times at Lou Yin, located five-tenths below the inner ankle, slightly above the pulse.[92p]
    Women may experience bleeding, with symptoms appearing through the years in response to their condition. The relevant acupuncture point is located beneath the big toe, at the junction of the flesh.

    Women who suffer from hernias and abdominal swelling may also have issues with diarrhea, urinary incontinence, painful lower regions, a darkened complexion, and pain under the eyes. For these symptoms, acupuncture at the Tai Chong point, penetrating three-tenths of an inch, can help, with the acupoint found two inches away from the base joint of the big toe.

    According to the "Ten Books of Li Gao": If the uterine opening does not close, and there is persistent bleeding, one should needle the Qi Men point, inserting five-tenths of an inch; this point is located three inches from the Guan Yuan point.

    The "Medical Essentials" states: For women with erratic menstrual flow and bleeding, one should use the Zhong Ji point, three-tenths of an inch deep, to nourish; additionally, the San Yin Jiao point should be needled at five-tenths of an inch.[93p]
    The "Principles of Diagnosis and Treatment" states: If menstrual blood is excessive and does not stop, along with metrorrhagia, use the points San Yin Jiao and Xing Jian, needling and then moxibustion. Additionally, use the point Tong Li by needling it and moxibustion 27 times; the point is located two inches from the tip of the little toe.

    Another method: for blood flooding and leakage, needle the Zhong Ji point to tonify it, locating it two and a half inches from the uterus.

    Another method: if there is persistent stagnant blood, use the points San Yin Jiao, Bai Lao, Feng Men, Zhong Ji, Shen Yu, Gao Huang, Qu Chi, and Jue Gu.

    For women experiencing leakage of bad blood and overall disharmony, or sudden and persistent flooding, with excessive discharge of watery substances, perform moxibustion on the Blood Sea point located on the Spleen meridian, Tai Yin, either 27 or 21 times, to immediately stop the issue.

    If menstrual blood is too abundant and appears dark and purplish, leading to severe flooding, accompanied by shortness of breath and extreme coldness around the navel and abdomen, resulting in profuse sweating and a very weak pulse, moxibustion on the Guan Yuan point should be performed 100 times, located three inches below the navel, in the center.[94p]
    Medical Case

    "The Confucian Gentleman's Filial Piety" states: A certain gentleman's mother, over fifty years of age, suffered from a blood collapse for a year. Many suggested remedies like Ze Lan Wan, Hei Shen San, Bao An Wan, and Bai Wei San, but none were effective. A physician observed that the heavenly essence had already diminished, and there should not be any bleeding; rather, the blood flowed due to excess heat, not cold. In women, blood collapse often arises from great sorrow and crying; excessive sadness can disrupt the lung and heart, leading to uncontrollable bleeding. The "Internal Classic" says: when yin is deficient and yang is excessive, it is called collapse. If the yin vessels are insufficient and the yang vessels are abundant, it will result in internal collapse and bleeding. The world treats it with tonics, but none recognize it as incorrect; thus, one may take large doses. The large dose is Huang Lian Jie Du Decoction. Additionally, two taels of Xiang Fu Zi, two taels of Bai Shao Yao, and one tael of Dang Gui were roasted and ground into a fine powder, then mixed with water to take; subsequently, taking Bing Lang Wan, she found peace within a few days.[95p]
    In the Western Garden, a woman aged sixty-two suffered from continuous vaginal bleeding. After administering four doses of Huanglian Jiedu Decoction, she took Liangge Powder combined with Siwu Decoction for six doses and was cured. This woman, overwhelmed by excessive grief, had her heart stressed and her lungs raised, resulting in a blockage in the upper burner and heat inside, leading to the bleeding; thus, the treatment was effective.

    In the "Ten Treatises of Li Gao," it is stated that in the middle of winter, Guo Dafang came and reported that his wife's menstruation had suddenly begun to bleed profusely and would not stop. Previously, she had lost blood due to bodily harm, and afterward, her cycle had been irregular, but now it had ceased entirely. This person was anxious, quick-tempered, and often startled, indicating to me that it was likely due to insufficient heart qi and irregular eating habits. Guo Dafang disagreed. Upon visiting her, he found her palms cold, and her pulse was deep, fine, and slow; upon inquiry, it was confirmed to be deep and rapid, with subtle issues in the nine orifices and weakness in the limbs, shortness of breath, and imbalance in mouth and nasal breath. There was indeed evidence of insufficient heart qi and weakness in the spleen and stomach, with pain in the epigastric area and tightness under the left rib, indicating accumulation. There was movement in the area around the navel, signs of abdominal rumbling, difficulty in bowel movements, and many indications of deficiency, which could not all be documented. The treatment should focus on addressing the root cause, and the remaining symptoms may then be resolved.[96p]
    Calm the mind and stabilize the spirit, settle the disturbances within, harmonize the spleen and stomach, greatly benefit the primordial qi, replenish the blood vessels, and nurture the spirit. Use a warming agent to dispel the winter’s cold that has settled in the skin, and add a small amount of medicine to reduce the fire at the life gate, preventing weakness in the limbs.

    Use one part Huanglian, three parts Shengdi, five parts each of Chao Shenchuo, Juhong, and Guizhi, and six parts of Caodoukou seeds. One qian each of Huangqi, Renshen, and Mahuang (without joints). One qian and five fen of Danggui body, and five pieces of Xingren, ground into a paste. Combine these and divide into two doses, with two and a half large bowls of water.

    Boil the Mahuang until foam disappears, then reduce to two bowls. Add all the herbs. Continue boiling until it reduces to one bowl. Take this between the hours of Si and Wu (around 9 AM to 1 PM) after food has completely digested, stopping after one dose.

    For stomach pain, this results from cold lodgings in the stomach, combine with large heat medicine, ingest fifteen pills of Caodoukou with plain water, and the pain will cease immediately. Afterwards, take liver accumulation medicine to remove the root cause of this accumulation for healing.[97p]
    A woman experienced irregular menstruation, with dark blood clots. There was a blood accumulation in the left side, and she had persistent diarrhea. Her food was sometimes undigested, and later, the blood clots were expelled along with fluid. This indicated that both yin aspects had been losing substantial blood for a long time. Even though her periods were still irregular, the diarrhea worsened to the point of three or four loose stools daily. After eating, she felt anxious, her appetite decreased, and she even became weak.

    The elder from Dongyuan said: The sage treats illness based on the principles of the four seasons—rising, falling, floating, and sinking. It is essential to consider the annual climate and not disrupt the harmony with the heavens; one must not be excessive or deficient, causing untimely death for people, nor allow external evils or lose the proper balance, which would jeopardize longevity. Thus, Zhongjing states: When yang is strong and yin is deficient, purging will bring recovery, while sweating will lead to death; when yin is strong and yang is deficient, sweating will restore health, but purging will cause death.

    In general, the laws established by sages suggest that, for instance, remedies that elevate yang or disperse are used to assist the ascent of yang energy during spring and summer, while reducing the cold, stagnant energy preserved during autumn and winter. This is the nature of the illness and should be treated accordingly, adhering to the principles of rising, falling, floating, and sinking. The energy of heaven and earth rises and falls with the seasons; thus, an illness that begins cannot be reversed. Therefore, the classics say: Aligning with heaven leads to prosperity, while opposing it leads to demise. Can one not be wary of this? The human body also goes through the four seasons; the energy of heaven and earth is not only external but mirrors the essence of the individual.[98p]
    Currently, the leakage of essence does not stop, indicating that the front yin's qi and blood have already been depleted. If there is continual leakage for several years, it means the back yin's qi and blood have sunk down and been lost. The back yin is responsible for tangible things, while the front yin is the doorway to essence and qi. If the lower part is exhausted, it signifies that the patient's overall qi and blood are suffering, consistently affected by the autumn and winter season's regulations, wherein yin governs destruction. Such conditions are classified as illnesses related to storage. Yang thrives and yin grows during spring and summer. In the human body, the factor causing qi to rise and float is the upward movement of grain qi. When a patient’s overall blood and qi do not flourish, and the grain qi cannot sustain, their muscles and flesh diminish, indicating that the energies of both yin and yang are on the verge of depleting entirely. When the two lower yin components have been lost, and qi and blood are about to deplete, even if hot signs are present, if the lower part has been long depleted, it is considered as cold. This sickness, having long been stagnant and fallen, has a significant buildup of cold and damp, thus urgent intervention is needed. Clear cold with heat, eliminate dampness with dryness, elevate and lift significantly to help growth, and nourish qi and blood to prevent complete depletion. The sage has established methods for treatment; since dampness is overwhelmingly strong, it should be treated with what it is defeated by, assisting with the upward rise of wood energy. Thus, it is written that wind conquers dampness; hence what is conquered is balanced. One must first regulate stomach qi, then use substances like white atractylodes to dry the dampness and nourish the primordial qi; if this does not cease, subsequently use wind-dispelling herbs to conquer dampness. This is a significant elevation and uplift to assist with the long-standing damp of spring and summer for proper treatment.[99p]
    The Marquis of Xuan-de, after experiencing the death of a family member due to a long-standing illness that no physician could cure, took the pulse and noted the symptoms on paper, identifying over forty. He formulated a remedy to regulate the menstruation, promote Yang, and eliminate moisture. The next day, the condition improved drastically, and after five or six days, he was fully recovered.

    The "Official Medical Text" states: An elderly woman, aged fifty-three, had been suffering from prolonged uterine bleeding that did not cease, and none of the medications were effective. I used two ingredients, the acorn and the root of the xanthium flower, burned to a viable state, and prepared a decoction from the Four Substances Decoction, including white atractylodes, bulrush flower, and dried ginger, to regulate her treatment.[100p]
    In the "Xue Clan Medical Cases," it is said: In the household of Grand Judge Wang Tiancheng, after long suffering from bleeding, he self-administered a cooling blood remedy, intermittently, yet his condition worsened due to increased anger, resulting in persistent bleeding. The previous remedies proved ineffective; thus, the focus shifted to reducing internal heat, accompanied by severe pain in the abdomen and chest, with cold hands and feet. I stated: This is caused by deficiency and cold in the spleen and stomach. First, apply Fuzi Li Zhong Decoction, which alleviated the pain due to internal heat. Next, the Jisheng Gui Pi and Bu Zhong Yi Qi Decoctions were used, leading to an immediate cessation of the bleeding. To ignore the pain without a tonifying method would be a mistake.

    In the household of Jin Yi Yang Yongxing, there was a case of bleeding; excessive consumption of cold remedies further exacerbated the condition, causing bloating and discomfort in the abdomen, loss of appetite, fever, and restlessness, with a rapid and weak pulse. I concluded: This is due to deficiency of qi and blood in the spleen meridian, leading to increased irritability. It is urgent to use Ba Zhen Decoction with roasted ginger to nourish and warm the body; delay could mean a loss of remedy. Disbelieving my words, they instead took a remedy to stop the bleeding and reduce heat, which only resulted in symptoms of deficiency rising. They finally believed my words, but it was too late to treat the condition effectively.

    A woman presented with a pale or flushed face, pain in the lower back or beneath the navel, fatigue in the limbs, feelings of heat and restlessness, experiencing alternating chills and fever with a sensation of constriction in the ribs, and blood surging as if it would overflow. This was due to depletion of the spleen and stomach, leading to sinking of the vital energy, combined with damp heat from the fire elements. The treatment included Bu Zhong Yi Qi Decoction, with the addition of Fangfeng, Shaoyao, and roasted Huangbai, interspersed with Gui Pi Decoction for adjustment and nourishment, enabling the blood to return to the proper channels.[101p]
    A woman suffered from heavy bleeding due to anger, with her face turning pale and sometimes reddish. This indicated that the liver's wood was overpowering the spleen's earth, leading to blood deficiency. Small Chai Hu was combined with Si Wu to clear liver fire and nourish liver blood. Additionally, Gui Pi and Bu Zhong Yi Qi Decoction were used to strengthen spleen qi and generate liver blood, resulting in recovery. If the condition was due to wind-heat in the liver channel, Fang Feng was prescribed in pill form, along with decoction for concurrent symptoms. If there was liver fire causing blood instability, stir-fried Qiao was also used in pill form along with decoction for concurrent symptoms. If blood stasis was the issue, Wu Ling Zhi was ground into powder and mixed with burnt iron and wine for consumption, yielding effective results.

    One woman, after eating, suddenly suffered from blood loss due to anger. Her limbs were cold, she experienced spasms, and her mouth was tightly shut, resembling a convulsion with sputum erupting. She was treated with Er Chen, Chai Hu, San Zhi, and Zhi Ke, prompting her to expel sour taste, and her spirit improved slightly. As the medicine was paused, on the next day she consumed a small amount of thin porridge but still experienced breast and rib discomfort, chills and heat, nausea, and fatigue in her limbs. My view was that this was entirely due to excessive liver fire preventing spleen qi from functioning. I initially used Liu Jun, Chai Hu, San Zhi, and Diao Teng Gou to address various symptoms, but her limbs remained unresponsive and the bleeding persisted. It seemed that the liver fire hadn’t subsided, and I considered administering a formula to clear the liver and cool the blood. I believed both the liver and spleen's qi and blood were weak, so I first used Bu Zhong Yi Qi Decoction to nourish the spleen’s earth and restore blood qi. Additionally, I applied Si Wu, Ren Shen, Bai Zhu, and Chai Hu to nurture the liver and relax the tendons, allowing her limbs to function better.[102p]
    A woman had long suffered from excessive uterine bleeding, her limbs were emaciated, and upon eating, she would smell a fishy odor.
